stem: You are the emergency department resident assessing a 4-month-old boy brought in with a 3-day coryzal illness and worsening cough. He has taken only half his usual feeds for the last 12 hours and has not had a wet nappy for 8 hours. He was born at term with no comorbidities. His 3-year-old sister had a cold last week. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. No vaccines are overdue.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 °C, HR 168, RR 62, SpO2 92% room air
data_table.Examination: Subcostal and intercostal recession, fine crackles bilaterally with wheeze, mild head bobbing, alert and tracking carers
data_table.Investigations: Nasopharyngeal swab pending; routine bloods and chest x-ray not indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg via mask with reassessment of respiratory work in 20 minutes
options.rationale: Salbutamol does not improve outcomes in viral bronchiolitis in infants under 12 months and is not recommended in the Australian paediatric pathway. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg single dose with planned review in the department in 4 hours
options.rationale: Systemic corticosteroids do not shorten bronchiolitis or reduce admission length and are not recommended by Australian guidelines. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Admit to the paediatric ward for nasal high-flow oxygen and supported feeding via nasogastric tube
options.rationale: Sustained SpO2 92% with significant work of breathing, tachypnoea above 60 and feeding under 50% meet admission criteria; nasal high-flow improves oxygenation and reduces escalation. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with written red flag advice and follow-up by GP within 24 hours
options.rationale: Hypoxia, severe work of breathing and feeding under half are explicit admission triggers; discharge risks overnight deterioration and apnoea. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence intravenous benzylpenicillin and ceftriaxone with admission for monitoring
options.rationale: There is no clinical evidence of bacterial co-infection and empirical antibiotics are not part of the bronchiolitis pathway. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis in children under 12 months driven by viral lower respiratory tract infection. The Royal Children's Hospital guideline indicates ward admission for sustained SpO2 under 92%, severe work of breathing, feeding under 50% of normal, apnoea or social concerns. Nasal high-flow oxygen is the standard escalation when low-flow oxygen and feeding support are inadequate. Bronchodilators, corticosteroids and antibiotics are not part of the standard pathway.
key_points: SpO2 below 92% triggers oxygen support admission.
Feeding below half of normal is an admission criterion.
Nasal high-flow is the preferred escalation in moderate disease.
Bronchodilators, steroids and antibiotics are not routine.
explanation.pearls: RSV-positive infants under 3 months and those born before 32 weeks are at highest risk of apnoea; admit even if SpO2 appears adequate, because apnoea may be the first sign of severe disease in this group.

stem: You are the after-hours emergency department registrar reviewing a 2-year-old boy brought in with one day of coryza and a barking cough that started this evening. He has stridor audible at rest and mild intercostal recession but is interactive, smiling at his parents and has good colour. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. There are no immunisation gaps and no regular medications.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.4 °C, HR 130, RR 36, SpO2 96% room air
data_table.Examination: Biphasic stridor at rest with mild intercostal recession, no cyanosis, no agitation, alert and interactive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg via mask and reassess respiratory effort in 20 minutes
options.rationale: Salbutamol treats lower airway bronchospasm rather than the upper airway narrowing of croup and does not relieve stridor. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 0.5 mL/kg of 1 in 1000 and prepare for paediatric intubation
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is indicated for severe croup with marked respiratory distress or impending failure, not moderate disease with preserved alertness. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

options.text: Intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg now and admit for intravenous antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Bacterial tracheitis presents with high fever and toxic appearance; this child is well between cough bouts and antibiotics are not indicated. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg single dose and observe in the department for 4 hours
options.rationale: Moderate croup with stridor at rest but no severe distress is treated with a single dose of oral dexamethasone and 4-hour observation; most children improve and are safely discharged. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

options.text: Endotracheal intubation in theatre with ENT and anaesthetics teams on standby
options.rationale: Intubation is reserved for impending or actual respiratory failure with cyanosis, exhaustion or reduced responsiveness, none of which is present. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

explanation.summary: Croup severity is graded clinically as mild (stridor with activity), moderate (stridor at rest with mild recession) or severe (stridor at rest with marked distress or altered mental state). Australian first-line treatment for moderate croup is a single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg followed by 4-hour observation. Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for severe disease and bridges the time to dexamethasone onset. Antibiotics, bronchodilators and intubation are not used in uncomplicated viral croup.
key_points: Stridor at rest without severe distress defines moderate croup.
Single-dose oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg is first-line.
Observe for 4 hours after steroid and discharge if stable.
Reserve adrenaline for severe croup or impending failure.
explanation.pearls: Rebound at 4 to 6 hours after a single adrenaline dose is well described; never discharge a child who needed nebulised adrenaline without 4 hours of clear observation after administration, regardless of how well they look immediately afterwards.

stem: You are the GP reviewing an 18-month-old girl brought in by her mother with a 2-day history of fever and irritability, now refusing to lie flat and tugging at both ears. She is otherwise well, immunisations are up to date, and the family is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. She has no eczema, no rash, and no recorded penicillin allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.2 °C, HR 150, RR 30, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Both tympanic membranes red and bulging with absent light reflex; neck supple; chest clear; no rash; no neck stiffness

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 30 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days plus paracetamol for analgesia
options.rationale: Bilateral acute otitis media in a child under 2 years with high fever and severe pain warrants immediate amoxicillin; 30 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days is the Therapeutic Guidelines recommendation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Watchful waiting with paracetamol and review by phone in 48 hours if no improvement
options.rationale: Watchful waiting suits unilateral mild disease in children over 2 years, not bilateral disease with high fever in a child under 2. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Topical chloramphenicol ear drops for both ears with daily fluids and analgesia
options.rationale: Topical antibiotic drops have no role in middle-ear infection through an intact tympanic membrane; they target external otitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral pseudoephedrine for 7 days with paracetamol and ibuprofen alternating for pain
options.rationale: Decongestants do not reduce middle-ear effusion or shorten illness and carry significant adverse effects in young children. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg for 3 days with delayed antibiotic prescription if needed
options.rationale: Systemic steroids are not part of acute otitis media management in Australian guidelines and may mask deterioration. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Therapeutic Guidelines recommend immediate antibiotic therapy for acute otitis media in children with bilateral disease under 2 years, perforation with discharge, systemic illness, immunosuppression or Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander background. Amoxicillin 30 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days is first-line; amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for treatment failure at 48 to 72 hours. Adequate analgesia with paracetamol and ibuprofen is core to management irrespective of antibiotic decision. Watchful waiting suits uncomplicated unilateral disease in older children.
key_points: Children under 2 with bilateral disease need antibiotics now.
Amoxicillin 30 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days is first-line.
Analgesia is essential regardless of antibiotic choice.
Switch to amoxicillin-clavulanate if no improvement at 48-72 h.
explanation.pearls: Recurrent acute otitis media (three or more episodes in 6 months or four in 12 months) prompts ENT referral for grommet consideration; mastoiditis presents with post-auricular swelling, tenderness and downward-and-outward displacement of the pinna and warrants urgent admission.

stem: You are the GP seeing a 3-year-old girl brought by her mother with a 3-month history of hard infrequent stools every 5 to 7 days, soiling episodes and stool withholding posture during the day. There are no red flags: no blood per rectum, no abdominal distension, normal growth, normal back and anus on examination, and no delayed meconium at birth. The family is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Examination: Soft palpable faecal mass in the left lower quadrant; normal anus and back; normal lower limb neurology; growth on the 50th centile

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Lactulose 1 mL/kg twice daily as the sole pharmacological treatment for 2 weeks
options.rationale: Lactulose is acceptable but Australian guidelines now recommend macrogol 3350 first-line because of higher disimpaction success and better palatability. (Source: RCH CPG Constipation)

options.text: Macrogol 3350 with electrolytes disimpaction regimen followed by daily maintenance dosing
options.rationale: Macrogol disimpaction with up to 8 sachets per day for up to 7 days followed by maintenance is the first-line Australian regimen for functional constipation with faecal loading. (Source: RCH CPG Constipation)

options.text: Daily oral senna alone with dietary fibre advice and toilet sitting after meals
options.rationale: Stimulants are added if osmotics alone fail; routine first-line use can cause cramping and is not standard for initial therapy. (Source: RCH CPG Constipation)

options.text: Refer to paediatric surgery for examination under anaesthetic and inpatient bowel washout
options.rationale: Surgical referral is reserved for organic causes such as Hirschsprung disease or anorectal malformation; none of these features are present here. (Source: RCH CPG Constipation)

options.text: Daily PR phosphate enemas for one week and then dietary fibre advice alone
options.rationale: Routine PR therapy is invasive, traumatising for young children and not first-line for typical functional constipation in well children. (Source: RCH CPG Constipation)

explanation.summary: Functional constipation with faecal loading is the commonest paediatric bowel complaint in Australia. Royal Children's Hospital and RACGP advice favour macrogol 3350 first-line for both disimpaction and maintenance. Disimpaction uses escalating sachet doses for up to 7 days, then maintenance is titrated to achieve one soft stool per day. Long-term success depends on toileting routine, reward charts and continued maintenance for at least 6 months after symptom resolution.
key_points: Macrogol 3350 is first-line for disimpaction and maintenance.
Disimpaction escalates over 7 days; maintenance follows.
Maintain therapy for at least 6 months after symptoms resolve.
Reserve surgery referral for organic red flags.
explanation.pearls: Beware of stopping macrogol the moment stools normalise; relapse rates are high without 6 months of maintenance, and many families need explicit permission to keep dosing once everything looks better.

subtopic_detail: Household pertussis chemoprophylaxis with infant under 6 months
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 32-year-old mother with 3 weeks of paroxysmal cough and post-tussive vomiting. Pertussis PCR on a nasopharyngeal swab is positive. She lives with her 6-week-old infant who received the 6-week DTPa-IPV-Hib-HepB dose 3 days ago, a healthy 4-year-old who is up to date with vaccines, and her partner who had Tdap 12 years ago. None are Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: Mother: HR 76, BP 118/74, SpO2 98% room air; infant examined separately and well
data_table.Examination: Mother coughs intermittently during consultation; infant is feeding well with no apnoea, no cough and no cyanosis
data_table.Investigations: Maternal pertussis PCR positive; infant pertussis PCR not yet sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate post-exposure plan for the household?

options.text: Treat the mother with azithromycin and provide red flag advice only to the other household members
options.rationale: Pertussis spreads readily within households; chemoprophylaxis is indicated for all contacts when an infant under 6 months lives in the home regardless of vaccination status. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Vaccinate the 6-week-old with an accelerated DTPa dose and the adults with Tdap boosters
options.rationale: Vaccination does not provide rapid post-exposure protection; chemoprophylaxis is required for vulnerable contacts within the household. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Test the 4-year-old and partner with PCR first and treat only those who return a positive result
options.rationale: Pre-test triage delays prophylaxis and risks transmission to the infant during the 24- to 48-hour wait for swab results. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Defer chemoprophylaxis pending the public health unit's instructions on close contacts
options.rationale: Public health notification is mandatory, but commencing prophylaxis should not wait; treatment of contacts within 21 days of exposure to an infant is time-critical. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Give azithromycin chemoprophylaxis to the infant, sibling and adults alongside treating the mother
options.rationale: When an infant under 6 months lives in the household, azithromycin chemoprophylaxis is given to all close household contacts to prevent severe and fatal infant pertussis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Pertussis remains a serious risk in young infants, with mortality concentrated in those under 6 months. Australian Therapeutic Guidelines indicate chemoprophylaxis for all close household contacts when an infant under 6 months lives in the home, irrespective of vaccination status. Azithromycin is first-line because of short course and good compliance. The index case is notifiable and the public health unit will trace wider contacts, but treatment should not be delayed for notification.
key_points: Index case requires treatment and notification.
All household contacts get prophylaxis when an infant under 6 months lives in the home.
Azithromycin is the first-line chemoprophylaxis agent.
Vaccination alone does not protect close contacts in time.
explanation.pearls: Infants under 6 months can present with apnoea before cough, especially if exposed to a coughing adult at home; admit for monitoring at the threshold of any apnoea, cyanosis or feeding change even if pertussis PCR has not yet returned.
